Summary: During perinatal lung development prior to birth, NKX2-1 regulates transcriptional targets as SOX9 progenitors differentiate into alveolar type 2 (AT2) cells. NKX2-1 is required in AT2 cells to induce diverse transcriptional targets and it directly regulates genes encoding surfactant proteins A, B, C, and D and lipid‑homeostasis genes Abca3, Scd1, and Sreb7. NKX2-1 activates targets that drive surfactant lipid, surfactant protein, and innate host defense protein synthesis in partnership with FOXA1/2, KLF5, GATA6, TEAD, SREBF1, NF1, and CEBPα binding cis‑regulatory elements. NKX2-1 phosphorylation and changes in chromatin accessibility modulate NKX2-1 activity at those targets. As a consequence, NKX2-1 transcriptional targets increase dramatically before birth without increased NKX2-1 expression. Epigenomic modulation via PRDM3 and PRDM16 mediates NKX2-1-dependent perinatal AT2 cell differentiation and surfactant homeostasis by regulating chromatin accessibility at NKX2-1 targets. Combined deletion of Prdm3 and Prdm16 in early lung endoderm causes perinatal lethality from respiratory failure due to loss of AT2 cells and accumulation of partially differentiated AT1 cells, and lineage-specific deletion of PRDM3/16 in AT2 cells causes lineage infidelity with PRDM3/16-null cells acquiring a partial AT1 fate.
References: He, H., Bell, S. M., Davis, A. K., Zhao, S., Sridharan, A., Na, C. L., Guo, M., Xu, Y., Snowball, J., Swarr, D. T., Zacharias, W. J., & Whitsett, J. A. (2024). PRDM3/16 regulate chromatin accessibility required for NKX2-1 mediated alveolar epithelial differentiation and function. Nat Commun, 15(1), 8112. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-024-52154-3
